## [00:00 - 03:02]

In this market validation lesson what we're going to look at is how to fill the pool from the market, how to validate that market and how to really scale that market. So it's important to validate a new market for a number of reasons. One for me is that a lot of the time people are thinking growth has to come from a new product but that's not necessarily true. Sometimes the easiest growth is taking your existing product and putting it into a new market and one of the keys for me to do that successfully is not to go off your gut, it's to fill the market pool. If you've got product X doing well in market X and then you fill a pool from market Y for product X and you may have expensive shipping rates, not very good delivery, all these things but you start to see people from that market want what you have, that's the perfect time to go okay we need to look at this market, we need to see if we did that market properly, can it match our existing market, is a chance it will even beat your existing market. So for me that's where you're swimming downstream, you're filling that market pool and you're going into it because that's where scale can be. So in the market validation lesson probably the first step is finding that market with the least resistance, whether it's filling the pool from the market which is great but not always going to be there in some aspects. So another way to do it and we've done this before is just go wide with a campaign advertising to a region, it could be Europe, it could be Asia, it could be the US, it could be certain states in the US and then look at the results you're getting, look at results, look at where you're getting reasonable customer acquisition costs, look at where you're getting better than average conversion costs, conversion rates on your website, that may be a market that you want to have a closer look at and run further tests to try and validate is this a market that has enough scale, enough headroom for us to grow in and are there enough people there that are interested in what we're doing. So the cheap clicks and the conversion rate generally signs that you have is a good product market fit. Now you're doing this usually before you've gone and fixed the upside of the business so you won't have gone and invested in awesome delivery and scalable delivery or anything like that on the ground, 3PL warehouses but what you're doing is you're probably just selling these products at a loss, getting them to the customer but learning and getting knowledge and that's the valuable thing here because that is the thing that goes okay, there's success could be here and it looks like it's going to be here. From everything we can tell this will work and at that point you go and then invest the time and energy in setting up a 3PL, holding stock on the ground there and then selling to them but before that you need to work out okay we've got to pull from the market, we think it's going to work, does the economics work, can we get product there cheaply, can we deliver that product there at affordable price with a good service that customers are

## [03:02 - 06:02]

going to be happy with and are we taking in all the duties, taxes, land and costs, everything that comes along with that so before you do all that work you may as well know there will be success here if the rest of it works out. So when validating a new market a common mistake I see people make is they judge the initial results without giving thought to scale so often what happens is when you go to market with something new you get what is called the low hanging fruit and so you're picking all that low hanging fruit and you're thinking this is going to be amazing, the problem with seeing that is these are the early adopters, these are the people who are the perfect customer, chances are there's not enough of the perfect customer so you need to go to the next rung of customers that is where every person is and find that big chunk of the market where you can go yeah there's something there and I can get the early ones, they'll be cheap and they'll come on board and they're going to love the product but then I can go to the next lot, a little bit more expensive but still enough people to make all the effort worth it and scale the business. So recently we validated a new market by opening up quadlock.asia website and to do that we ran really wide Facebook ads at the Asian region as a whole and from there we saw lots of data coming back on cheap clicks, cheap customer acquisition costs, some countries would check out on the website better than others and a lot of this comes down to language, payment options, all these kind of things that you can't, one website doesn't necessarily translate to the other whereas in some places cash on deliveries are a big thing and we don't have a way of offering that at scale in other places, people are happy to play with credit card PayPal so you've got to make sure that it can be that the market's good but your website that you've got initially may not just be fitting that market and once you can learn that and get that feedback from the customers you can then start tailoring your website and your offering to that new market and it's not a new product, it's not a new anything really, all it is is better servicing that market and another point to it is that we saw really cheap clicks coming from places like India, the thing was there's such a two step economy there that we were getting good conversions on one hand but not as many as to how many clicks we were getting so that pros is a problem that we're spending a lot of money on thousands and thousands of clicks without getting the return on investment on that money so at that time it's like okay there's a good market there maybe if we can penetrate the right customer base there but there may be a better market right now in the Philippines so we can turn that off segment it out maybe not market to it right now go and nail these other few countries that we're having great success like Singapore, Malaysia these kind of places and then come back to that at a later date. So now it's up to you to validate a new market using Facebook and write the results in the workbook provided and we'll catch you in the next lesson.
